answer: The 80th Regiment of Light-Armed Foot was raised in May 1758; The 80th Regiment of Foot (Staffordshire Volunteers) was an infantry regiment of the British Army, raised in December 1793. Under the Childers Reforms it amalgamated with the 38th (1st Staffordshire) Regiment of Foot to form the South Staffordshire Regiment in 1881. Its lineage is continued today by the 3rd Battalion, Mercian Regiment. The regiment was raised by Lord Henry Paget as the 80th Regiment of Foot, in response to the threat posed by the French Revolution, on 9 December 1793.

answer:
The creator depends on which work titled "The Wilderness Years" is referenced: it was written by Ferdinand Fairfax (with Martin Gilbert as co-writer) for a 1981 drama serial about Winston Churchill [RAM0308-D06], or by Sue Townsend for the fourth book in the Adrian Mole series published in 1993 [RAM0308-D01]
Additionally, "The Wilderness Years" is an album created by British singer-songwriter Nick Lowe released in 1991 [RAM0308-D04], while a work titled *Winston Churchill: The Wilderness Years*—an eight-part drama serial based on the period from 1929 to 1939 with historian Martin Gilbert as co-writer [RAM0308-D06]—corresponds to a section of Martin Gilbert's biography of Churchill covering that same decade, specifically aligning with the latter half of volume five which deals roughly with the years 1929 to 1939 [RAM0308-D08].

answer:

No. 94 Squadron RAF was formed at Harling Road on 1 August 1917 as a training unit for the Sopwith Camel; it mobilised in May 1918, moved to France in October 1918, returned to the UK without seeing action due to the imminent end of World War I, and disbanded on 30 June 1919. The squadron was later reformed at Castlereagh, New South Wales, on 30 May 1945; it commenced training in July 1945 but did not complete its initial preparations before the end of World War II. Additionally, Fighter Squadron 94 (VF-94) was established at NAS Alameda, California, on March 26, 1952.
It has been formed a total of four times: the first formation occurred at RAF Harling Road in 1917

1627 – 31 December 1700 [RAM0370-D01] (Thomas Belasyse, 1st Earl Fauconberg) was an English peer who supported the Parliamentary cause in the English Civil War; he became close to Oliver Cromwell and married Cromwell's third daughter, Mary. After the Restoration of the monarchy he became a member of the Privy Council to Charles II and was elevated to an earldom by William III. Belasyse was the only son of Hon.
